title: Udinese Calcio

infobox.value: *I Bianconeri* (The White and Blacks)   *I Friulani* (The Friulians)   *Le Zebrette* (The Little Zebras)

* 30 November 1896; 129 years ago (30 November 1896) * as *Società Udinese di Ginnastica e Scherma.* * 5 July 1911; 114 years ago (5 July 1911) * as *Associazione del Calcio Udine* * 1919; 107 years ago (1919) * as *Associazione Sportiva Udinese* * 1925; 101 years ago (1925) * as *Associazione Calcio Udinese* * 1978; 48 years ago (1978) * as *Udinese Calcio*

(Italian pronunciation: [[udiˈneːze ˈkaltʃo, -eːse -]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); "Udinese Football") is a professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Udine](/wiki/Udine "Udine"), [Friuli-Venezia Giulia](/wiki/Friuli-Venezia_Giulia "Friuli-Venezia Giulia"), Italy. The team currently competes in the [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A"), the first tier of [Italian football](/wiki/Italian_football_league_system "Italian football league system"). It was founded on 30 November 1896 as a sports club, and on 5 July 1911 as a football club.

**Udinese Calcio** (Italian pronunciation: [[udiˈneːze ˈkaltʃo, -eːse -]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); "Udinese Football") is a professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Udine](/wiki/Udine "Udine"), [Friuli-Venezia Giulia](/wiki/Friuli-Venezia_Giulia "Friuli-Venezia Giulia"), Italy. The team currently competes in the [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A"), the first tier of [Italian football](/wiki/Italian_football_league_system "Italian football league system"). It was founded on 30 November 1896 as a sports club, and on 5 July 1911 as a football club.

The traditional team home kit is black and white striped shirt, black shorts, and white socks. The club broadcasts on channel 110 ([Udinese Channel](/wiki/Udinese_Channel "Udinese Channel")) on [digital terrestrial television](/wiki/Digital_terrestrial_television "Digital terrestrial television") in the north-east of Italy. It has many fans in [Friuli](/wiki/Friuli "Friuli") and the surrounding areas.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udinese_Calcio&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Foundation and early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udinese_Calcio&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Foundation and early years")]

Udinese Calcio was established in 1896 as part of the *Società Udinese di Ginnastica e Scherma*, (Udinese Society of Gymnastics and Fencing). In its inaugural year, the club won the Torneo FNGI in [Treviso](/wiki/Treviso "Treviso") beating Ferrara 2–0; however, this title is not recognised as official.

On 5 July 1911, some gymnasts of Udinese, headed by Luigi Dal Dan, founded the A.C. Udinese, which joined the [FIGC](/wiki/FIGC "FIGC"). The new side made its debut in a friendly match against [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_F.C. "Juventus F.C."), and won 6–0.

It was only in [1912–13](/wiki/Italian_Football_Championship_1912-13 "Italian Football Championship 1912-13") that Udinese first took part in an official FIGC championship. In that year they enrolled in the *Campionato Veneto di Promozione*, which consisted of just three teams (the others were Petrarca and Padova). With two victories against Padova (3–1 and 5–0), Udinese finished the tournament in second place behind Petrarca and were promoted to first-level [Prima Categoria](/wiki/Prima_Categoria "Prima Categoria"). In Prima Categoria, Udinese failed to reach the national stage, always knocked out in the *Eliminatoria Veneta*.

### 1920s: Coppa Italia final

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udinese_Calcio&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1920s: Coppa Italia final")]

The [1920–21](/wiki/Italian_Football_Championship_1920-21 "Italian Football Championship 1920-21") season, which ended with the Friulani eliminated in the *Eliminatoria Veneta*, was memorable because it was the debut of Gino Bellotto, who is still the player who has played the most seasons with Udinese, spending 17 seasons with the *Zebrette*.

In 1922, Udinese, taking advantage of the absence of big clubs, entered the [FIGC Italian Football Championship](/wiki/Italian_Football_Championship_1921-22_(F.I.G.C.) "Italian Football Championship 1921-22 (F.I.G.C.)") and reached the [Coppa Italia](/wiki/Coppa_Italia "Coppa Italia") final losing 1–0 against [Vado](/wiki/F.C._Vado "F.C. Vado"), thanks to an overtime goal.

In the league, Udinese finished second in *Girone Eliminatorio Veneto*, which allowed them to remain in the top flight for the next season, despite a reform of the championships that reduced the number of teams in the competition.

The [1922–23](/wiki/Italian_Football_Championship_1922-23 "Italian Football Championship 1922-23") season was a disastrous one for Udinese, as they came last in and were relegated to the second division. The team risked failure for debts in 1923. On 24 August 1923, AS Udinese separated from AC Udinese Friuli, and the club was forced to set up a budget and an autonomous board. All debts were paid by President Alessandro Del Torso through the sale of some of his paintings and Udinese could thus join the [Second Division](/wiki/Serie_B "Serie B") in which they came fourth.

The 1924–25 season was memorable. The team was included in Group F II Division. The championship was very even and at the end of the tournament three teams were in contention to win: Udinese, [Vicenza](/wiki/Vicenza_Calcio "Vicenza Calcio") and Olympia River. Playoffs were needed to determine who would reach the final round.

Udinese beat Olympia in a playoff 1–0 and drew 1–1 with Vicenza. In the play-off standings, Udinese and Vicenza were still in the lead with 3 points each. Another play-off was then played to determine the winner. After a first encounter finished 0–0, Udinese lost a replay 2–1 but were awarded the win as Vicenza fielded an ineligible player, a Hungarian called Horwart. Udinese reached the finals in place of Vicenza.

In the final round, Udinese finished first and was promoted, alongside [Parma](/wiki/Parma_F.C. "Parma F.C."), to [First Division](/wiki/Prima_Divisione "Prima Divisione"). In the following season, Udinese finished 10th and was relegated again. However, the format of the championship was again reformed and Udinese had another chance to reclaim their place in the top flight. They competed in play-offs with seven other sides for the right to play in Serie A. The winner would remain in the top flight. The club, however, lost the playoff against Legnano and lost their place in the top flight.

They remained in Second Division until the end of the 1928–29 season when [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") and [Serie B](/wiki/Serie_B "Serie B") were created, with Udinese falling into the third tier (Terza Serie). The first season in Terza Serie was a triumphant one and Udinese were promoted up to Serie B.

### 1930s and 1940s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udinese_Calcio&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1930s and 1940s")]

The stay in Serie B lasted only two years, and after the 1931–32 season, the team returned to the third division. Udinese remained in the third tier (later renamed [Serie C](/wiki/Serie_C "Serie C") in 1935) until 1938–39, when coming second in Girone Finale Nord di Serie C, they were promoted to Serie B.

The Zebrette remained in Serie B for a dozen years, with average performances and were relegated to Serie C at the end of the 1947–48 season due to a reform of the championships. This relegation, however, was followed by two consecutive promotions, and thanks to an excellent second-place finish in the [Serie B 1949-50](/wiki/Serie_B_1949-50 "Serie B 1949-50"), the Friulani won a historic promotion to [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A").

### 1950s: second place in A, and relegation back to B

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udinese_Calcio&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1950s: second place in A, and relegation back to B")]

Udinese remained in Serie A for five seasons and almost claimed an historic Scudetto in the [1954–55](/wiki/Serie_A_1954-55 "Serie A 1954-55") season, when they came second only behind [Milan](/wiki/A.C._Milan "A.C. Milan"). It was after that season, however, that Udinese was relegated because of an offence committed on 31 May 1953, the last day of the championship, which was exposed two years later. The Friuliani returned to Serie A after one season in B and in the following season was confirmed among the best Italian teams with an excellent fourth-place finish.

### 1960s and 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udinese_Calcio&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1960s and 1970s")]

A decline followed those good seasons, however, with Udinese first relegated back down to Serie B in 1961–62 and then to Serie C in 1963–64. Udinese remained in C for about fifteen years, missing promotion back to B on numerous occasions. It was only after the 1977–78 season that the Friuliani, led by manager [Massimo Giacomini](/wiki/Massimo_Giacomini "Massimo Giacomini"), returned to B winning Girone A. In the same season, they won the *Coppa Italia Semiprofessionisti*, beating [Reggina](/wiki/Reggina_Calcio "Reggina Calcio") and also won the [Anglo-Italian Cup](/wiki/Anglo-Italian_Cup "Anglo-Italian Cup").

### 1980s: Mitropa Cup and the scandal of 1986

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udinese_Calcio&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1980s: Mitropa Cup and the scandal of 1986")]

During the next season, Udinese with [Massimo Giacomini](/wiki/Massimo_Giacomini "Massimo Giacomini") as their manager, won [Serie B](/wiki/Serie_B "Serie B") and returned after more than two decades to [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A"). In their first year back after so long, the team survived after a disappointing 15th-place finish. In Europe, they fared much better, winning the [Mitropa Cup](/wiki/Mitropa_Cup "Mitropa Cup"), a European Cup for teams that had won the previous season of Serie B.

In subsequent seasons the team managed to survive relegation without any particular difficulty also managing an impressive sixth place in [1982–83](/wiki/Serie_A_1982-83 "Serie A 1982-83"). At that time Udinese had on its books one of the club's all-time greatest players, the Brazilian midfielder [Zico](/wiki/Zico_(footballer) "Zico (footballer)").

At the end of the [1985–86](/wiki/Serie_A_1985-86 "Serie A 1985-86") season, the team was embroiled in a betting scandal and was penalised nine points for the [1986–87](/wiki/Serie_A_1986-87 "Serie A 1986-87") season. Despite a desperate comeback towards the end of the season, Udinese were relegated to [Serie B](/wiki/Serie_B "Serie B"). Had they not been deducted points, Udinese would have survived.

### 1990s and early 2000s: Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udinese_Calcio&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1990s and early 2000s: Europe")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Stadio_Friuli.JPG/250px-Stadio_Friuli.JPG)](/wiki/File:Stadio_Friuli.JPG)

[Dacia Arena](/wiki/Stadio_Friuli "Stadio Friuli") before a Champions League match

During the following years, Udinese were promoted to Serie A and relegated back to B on several occasions. This situation lasted until the [1995–96](/wiki/Serie_A_1995-96 "Serie A 1995-96") season, from which point on, they established themselves in Serie A.

The [1996–97](/wiki/Serie_A_1996-97 "Serie A 1996-97") season saw Udinese qualify for the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup"), with [Alberto Zaccheroni](/wiki/Alberto_Zaccheroni "Alberto Zaccheroni") as manager. The following season, they managed a third-place finish behind [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_F.C. "Juventus F.C.") and [Internazionale](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan"), largely thanks to [Oliver Bierhoff](/wiki/Oliver_Bierhoff "Oliver Bierhoff")'s 27 goals.

In March 2001, [Luciano Spalletti](/wiki/Luciano_Spalletti "Luciano Spalletti") was appointed manager, replacing [Luigi De Canio](/wiki/Luigi_De_Canio "Luigi De Canio"). Spalletti managed to lead the team to survival on the penultimate matchday. Following brief periods with [Roy Hodgson](/wiki/Roy_Hodgson "Roy Hodgson") and [Giampiero Ventura](/wiki/Giampiero_Ventura "Giampiero Ventura") on the bench, Spalletti was again appointed manager of Udinese at the beginning of the [2002–03](/wiki/Serie_A_2002-03 "Serie A 2002-03") season, finding an organised and ambitious club which again reached the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup"), playing attacking and entertaining football.

The surprising fourth-place finish at the end of the [2004–05](/wiki/Serie_A_2004-05 "Serie A 2004-05") season saw Udinese achieve their first qualification for the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") in the history of the club. At the end of that same season, Spalletti announced his intention to leave Udinese.

The following season, Udinese played in the Champions League preliminary round, beating [Sporting CP](/wiki/Sporting_Clube_de_Portugal "Sporting Clube de Portugal") 4–2 on aggregate. Udinese were drawn in a tough group alongside [Panathinaikos](/wiki/Panathinaikos_F.C. "Panathinaikos F.C."), [Werder Bremen](/wiki/SV_Werder_Bremen "SV Werder Bremen") and [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Udinese2008.jpg/250px-Udinese2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Udinese2008.jpg)

[Mauricio Isla](/wiki/Mauricio_Isla "Mauricio Isla") (left) and [Alexis Sánchez](/wiki/Alexis_S%C3%A1nchez "Alexis Sánchez") (right) playing for Udinese in the UEFA Cup

Despite a 3–0 win over [Panathinaikos](/wiki/Panathinaikos_F.C. "Panathinaikos F.C.") in their first match, courtesy of a [Vincenzo Iaquinta](/wiki/Vincenzo_Iaquinta "Vincenzo Iaquinta") [hat trick](/wiki/Hat_trick "Hat trick"), the team failed to qualify for the knockout rounds, coming in third in their group, equal on points with second placed Werder and behind eventual champions Barcelona.

### Recent history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udinese_Calcio&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Recent history")]

After a year in the Champions League, Udinese finished tenth and returned once more to mid-table mediocrity. The turning point occurred during the summer of 2007, when the club announced the appointment of [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") manager [Pasquale Marino](/wiki/Pasquale_Marino "Pasquale Marino") and also made various quality purchases including [Fabio Quagliarella](/wiki/Fabio_Quagliarella "Fabio Quagliarella") and [Gökhan Inler](/wiki/G%C3%B6khan_Inler "Gökhan Inler").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Dinatale_v_Arsenal.jpg/250px-Dinatale_v_Arsenal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dinatale_v_Arsenal.jpg)

Striker Antonio Di Natale was the club's captain from 2007 until his retirement in 2016.

The [2007–08 season](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Serie_A "2007–08 Serie A") started well with a draw at home against champions [Internazionale](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan"), but the enthusiasm was quickly erased after the first home match which finished in a 5–0 loss to newly promoted [Napoli](/wiki/S.S.C._Napoli "S.S.C. Napoli"). After this match, Udinese's fortunes changed, starting with a victory over [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_F.C. "Juventus F.C.") thanks to a late [Antonio Di Natale](/wiki/Antonio_Di_Natale "Antonio Di Natale") goal. Udinese remained in contention for the fourth Champions League spot with [Milan](/wiki/A.C._Milan "A.C. Milan"), [Fiorentina](/wiki/ACF_Fiorentina "ACF Fiorentina"), and [Sampdoria](/wiki/U.C._Sampdoria "U.C. Sampdoria") until the end of the season, but ultimately finished in seventh place, qualifying for the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_UEFA_Cup "2008–09 UEFA Cup").

At the start of the [2008–09 season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Serie_A "2008–09 Serie A"), during the press conference to present the new season's kit, the new official website was also presented, and the first Web TV channel dedicated to a football club called Udinese Channel was launched.

In the 2008–09 season, Udinese had a mixed bag of results in Serie A with a 3–1 win at [Roma](/wiki/A.S._Roma "A.S. Roma") and a 2–1 win over [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_F.C. "Juventus F.C."), but 10 losses against teams including [Reggina](/wiki/Reggina_Calcio "Reggina Calcio"), [Chievo](/wiki/A.C._ChievoVerona "A.C. ChievoVerona"), and [Torino](/wiki/Torino_F.C. "Torino F.C.") dented their hopes of Champions League qualification. In the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_UEFA_Cup "2008–09 UEFA Cup"), Udinese found themselves in a group with potential favourites [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C."), [NEC](/wiki/NEC_Nijmegen "NEC Nijmegen"), [Spartak Moscow](/wiki/FC_Spartak_Moscow "FC Spartak Moscow"), and [Dinamo Zagreb](/wiki/GNK_Dinamo_Zagreb "GNK Dinamo Zagreb"), but eased through the group with a convincing 2–0 win against Tottenham. They beat [Lech Poznań](/wiki/Lech_Pozna%C5%84 "Lech Poznań") in the next round 4–3 on aggregate, and then beat holders [Zenit Saint Petersburg](/wiki/FC_Zenit_Saint_Petersburg "FC Zenit Saint Petersburg") 2–1 on aggregate. In the quarter-final against [Werder Bremen](/wiki/SV_Werder_Bremen "SV Werder Bremen"), with injuries to star players Antonio Di Natale, [Samir Handanovič](/wiki/Samir_Handanovi%C4%8D "Samir Handanovič"), and [Felipe](/wiki/Felipe_Dias_da_Silva_dal_Belo "Felipe Dias da Silva dal Belo"), they lost 6–4 on aggregate. [Fabio Quagliarella](/wiki/Fabio_Quagliarella "Fabio Quagliarella") managed eight goals in the campaign. They finished the season in seventh place, missing out on any European football the following year.

The [2009–10 season](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Serie_A "2009–10 Serie A") was an extremely disappointing one for players and fans alike. Even though Antonio Di Natale managed to score 29 goals in the league and finished top goalscorer, the season was spent battling against relegation. In the end, they finished in 15th, nine points and three places clear of the relegation zone. The only highlight of the campaign was reaching the semi-final of the [Coppa Italia](/wiki/Coppa_Italia "Coppa Italia"), beating [Lumezzane](/wiki/A.C._Lumezzane "A.C. Lumezzane") in the round of 16, [Milan](/wiki/A.C._Milan "A.C. Milan") in the quarter-finals, and eventually losing 2–1 to [Roma](/wiki/A.S._Roma "A.S. Roma") on aggregate.

In the summer transfer window of 2010, Udinese sold [Gaetano D'Agostino](/wiki/Gaetano_D%27Agostino "Gaetano D'Agostino"), [Simone Pepe](/wiki/Simone_Pepe "Simone Pepe"), [Marco Motta](/wiki/Marco_Motta "Marco Motta"), and [Aleksandar Luković](/wiki/Aleksandar_Lukovi%C4%87 "Aleksandar Luković"). They also brought in players that proved to be the key to their success in the [2010–11 Serie A](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Serie_A "2010–11 Serie A"); [Mehdi Benatia](/wiki/Mehdi_Benatia "Mehdi Benatia") and [Pablo Armero](/wiki/Pablo_Armero "Pablo Armero"), a central defender and wingback, respectively. After a poor start to the season, losing their first four games and drawing the fifth, Udinese went on to record their highest points total in history and finished in fourth place, again earning themselves a spot in the [Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") qualifying round. Di Natale, with 28 goals, became the first back-to-back *[capocannoniere](/wiki/Capocannoniere "Capocannoniere")* since [Lazio](/wiki/S.S._Lazio "S.S. Lazio")'s [Giuseppe Signori](/wiki/Giuseppe_Signori "Giuseppe Signori") accomplished the feat in [1993](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_Serie_A "1992–93 Serie A") and [1994](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_Serie_A "1993–94 Serie A"). A 0–0 home draw with Milan on the final matchday secured the Champions League spot for Udinese. Coach [Francesco Guidolin](/wiki/Francesco_Guidolin "Francesco Guidolin") kept his promise of "dancing like Boateng" if they qualified for the Champions League and did a little jig in the middle of the pitch. In the [Coppa Italia](/wiki/Coppa_Italia "Coppa Italia"), Udinese lost to [Sampdoria](/wiki/U.C._Sampdoria "U.C. Sampdoria") in the round of 16 on penalties after the match ended 2–2.

The [2011–12 season](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Serie_A "2011–12 Serie A") continued in much the same fashion, even though Udinese lost three key players to larger clubs – [Alexis Sánchez](/wiki/Alexis_S%C3%A1nchez "Alexis Sánchez") to [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona"), [Gökhan Inler](/wiki/G%C3%B6khan_Inler "Gökhan Inler") to [Napoli](/wiki/S.S.C._Napoli "S.S.C. Napoli"), and [Cristián Zapata](/wiki/Cristi%C3%A1n_Zapata "Cristián Zapata") to [Villarreal](/wiki/Villarreal_CF "Villarreal CF"). In the Champions League qualifying round, Udinese were drawn against [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") and lost the away leg 1–0. At the [Stadio Friuli](/wiki/Stadio_Friuli "Stadio Friuli"), Udinese lost 2–1, 3–1 on aggregate, and entered the [Europa League](/wiki/2011-12_Europa_League "2011-12 Europa League") group stage, Antonio Di Natale missing a penalty that at the time would have taken Udinese through. Domestically, Udinese started strong but with their quality shown in defence, conceding the least of all teams after 15 games, only seven. For the second consecutive season, Udinese qualified for the Champions League, clinching third place on the final day of the season with a 2–0 away win against [Catania](/wiki/Calcio_Catania "Calcio Catania"). In the summer transfer window, key players [Kwadwo Asamoah](/wiki/Kwadwo_Asamoah "Kwadwo Asamoah") and [Mauricio Isla](/wiki/Mauricio_Isla "Mauricio Isla") were both sold to champions Juventus. The club failed to reach the group stage of the year's Champions League, however, losing on penalties after extra time to Portuguese club [SC Braga](/wiki/S.C._Braga "S.C. Braga"). [Antonio Di Natale](/wiki/Antonio_Di_Natale "Antonio Di Natale") scored 23 goals to record his third consecutive season with 20+ goals in Serie A.

Udinese started off the [2012–13 Serie A](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Serie_A "2012–13 Serie A") season in mixed form, with seven draws and three losses in their first thirteen games. However, starting in December the team began to pick up wins more frequently, concurrent with Di Natale finding the net on a regular basis. After a period of balancing wins with losses, the team went on a phenomenal eight game winning streak to end the season, with [Luis Muriel](/wiki/Luis_Muriel "Luis Muriel") emerging as a key player. Like the 2011–12 season, Di Natale again finished with 23 goals, becoming the first player since [Gabriel Batistuta](/wiki/Gabriel_Batistuta "Gabriel Batistuta"), of [Fiorentina](/wiki/ACF_Fiorentina "ACF Fiorentina"), to score 20 or more goals in four or more consecutive seasons.

Over the coming years, Udinese would go on to finish middle to lower table in [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A"). In the 2017–18 season, Udinese manager [Massimo Oddo](/wiki/Massimo_Oddo "Massimo Oddo") was sacked after the club lost 11 straight games. Oddo was then replaced by [Igor Tudor](/wiki/Igor_Tudor "Igor Tudor") who guided the club to safety away from the relegation places.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Colors and symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udinese_Calcio&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Colors and symbols")]

### Colors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udinese_Calcio&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Colors")]

The official colors of Udinese are white and black, the same as the [municipal](/wiki/Udine "Udine") [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms"), these have been present in the team's [kit](/wiki/Kit_(association_football) "Kit (association football)") since its beginnings, although their use has often varied between different styles. At the end of the 19th century, the newly formed *Società Udinese di Ginnastica e Scherma* wore a completely black shirt, adorned only with a white [five-pointed star](/wiki/Five-pointed_star "Five-pointed star") pinned to the center of the chest.[[2]](#cite_note-Welter-2)

In the 1910s, the shirt was split vertically in half between the two colors,[[2]](#cite_note-Welter-2) later, white became the dominant color, with black relegated to decorations.[[3]](#cite_note-udinesestory-3) Since the 1930s the typical black and white striped shirt has been almost permanently used, often accompanied by black shorts and socks,[[4]](#cite_note-anni3040-4) or by white shorts and black socks.[[5]](#cite_note-dopoguerra-5)

The only significant innovations came in the 1980s when the Friulian team's clothing diverged from tradition, presenting more imaginative solutions: notably, the white shirts with a black central bar used in the first part of the decade, followed by black shirts with a massive white stripe across them.[[6]](#cite_note-zico-6)[[2]](#cite_note-Welter-2) From the 1990s onwards, there was a gradual return to simplicity, although experiments like "curved" stripes along the upper edge were made in the mid-2000s.[[7]](#cite_note-storia-7)

As for away uniforms, these do not follow fixed patterns except for the solid color, with the club changing colors every year, using blue, yellow, orange, sky blue, pink, and even the same black and white as the home kit.[[2]](#cite_note-Welter-2)

### Official symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udinese_Calcio&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Official symbols")]

**Crest**

Throughout its history, Udinese has used numerous [crests](/wiki/Crest_(sports) "Crest (sports)") that share the colors and heraldry of the [Friulian city](/wiki/Udine "Udine"). In a photo taken around 1896,[[8]](#cite_note-8) likely during an FGNI Tournament, the city [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") appears on the chest, with a white star on a black shirt. However, there is not enough information to confirm if this was used officially or continuously over the years. Indeed, in later photos from the early 20th century, no emblem appears on the shirts.

The first official emblems date back to the 1950s, 60s, and 70s, but since they were not depicted on the shirts, it is difficult to determine the exact period. All these emblems depicted a shield filled with black and white vertical [stripes](/wiki/Stripe_(pattern) "Stripe (pattern)"), first with the inscription "AC Udinese" and later with the "ACU" (Associazione Calcistica Udinese) [monogram](/wiki/Monogram "Monogram"). The first emblem visible on the shirt appeared in 1979, depicting a [zebra](/wiki/Zebra "Zebra") inside a green circle. The second symbol, introduced in the early 1980s under President Lamberto Mazza, featured a white shield enclosing a black [chevron](/wiki/Chevron_(insignia) "Chevron (insignia)") — the same design that represents the city of [Udine](/wiki/Udine "Udine") — with the "Z" logo of [Zanussi](/wiki/Zanussi "Zanussi") (the company that owned the club at the time) below it and the lowercase inscription "udinese calcio".[[9]](#cite_note-9) Despite this being the official emblem, for the [1981-1982 season](/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_Serie_A "1981–82 Serie A"), the shirts displayed only the large "Z" on the chest.

The third emblem was a variation of the second one: after Zanussi's departure, following the club's acquisition by [Giampaolo Pozzo](/wiki/Giampaolo_Pozzo "Giampaolo Pozzo"), the "Z" was removed, and only the shield with the city's emblem remained. Then, between 1992 and 1995, the club introduced a circular emblem for the first time, with the city’s coat of arms in the center, surrounded by a gray circle.

Finally, in the [1995-1996 season](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_Serie_A "1995–96 Serie A"), the emblem currently in use made its debut: a shield surrounded by a circle, first gray, then black, and from the [2010-2011 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Udinese_Calcio_season "2010–11 Udinese Calcio season"), gray again, with two [laurel branches](/wiki/Laurel_branches "Laurel branches") wrapped around it.

Fans have repeatedly requested that the [Friulian eagle](/wiki/Flag_of_Friuli "Flag of Friuli") should be added to the black and white shirts, but the club has never accepted this request, partly due to existing regulations. In 2008, the club's decision to include the emblem of [Friuli-Venezia Giulia](/wiki/Friuli-Venezia_Giulia "Friuli-Venezia Giulia") on the shirts was strongly contested by fans, as it was perceived as the emblem of rival [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste") rather than representing only [Friuli](/wiki/Friuli "Friuli").

**Anthem**

The club's official anthem is "Vinci per noi" (Win for Us), performed by singer Connie Del Colle. The song is played before every home game at the [Stadio Friuli](/wiki/Stadio_Friuli "Stadio Friuli").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Previously, the anthem was "Alè Udin," performed by Dario Zampa. Its chorus became a distinctive chant of the Friulian fans.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
